coredata.dc:title: Effect of temperature, and immersion time on the inhibition performance of Q235 steel using spent coffee grounds derived carbon quantum dots: Electrochemical, spectroscopic and surface studies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Aslam, Ruby
Wang, Qihui
Aslam, Jeenat
Mobin, Mohammad
Hussain, Chaudhery Mustansar
Yan, Zhitao
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the anticorrosive properties of undoped, and zinc and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots derived from spent coffee grounds referred to as CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs respectively, for the protection of Q235B steel corrosion in a 5 % HCl solution. The synthesized carbon quantum dots (CDs) were characterized using Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), zeta potential, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Their anticorrosive performance was assessed through weight loss measurements, Open circuit potential (OCP), Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), Scanning Electron Microscopy/Energy dispersive Spectroscopy (SEM/EDS), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The results demonstrated that Zn,N-CCDs exhibit better inhibition efficiency i.e., 94.65 % at 150 ppm compared to CCDs which show 90.90 % at 300 ppm and 298 K. Moreover, 150 ppm of Zn,N-CCDs at a longer immersion time of up to 72 h at 313 K exhibited an inhibition performance of 98.33 % proving it as an efficient corrosion inhibitor to be used in acid pickling conditions. PDP analysis suggested that the Zn,N-CCDs reduce both anodic and cathodic dissolution processes. XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed the adsorption of CDs through heteroatoms present in the coffee grounds. Furthermore, surface morphological studies indicated significant adsorption of the Zn,N-CCDs on the metal surface, enhancing surface smoothness as compared to CCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corrosion inhibition
Waste management
Bio-based material
Metal protection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of undoped and Zn,N-doped CDs
Spectroscopic analysis: UV–vis, FTIR and XPS analysis
Weight loss measurement: effect of concentration, temperature and immersion time
Spectroscopic analysis: UV–vis, FTIR and XPS analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The information provided in Table 3 and S3 (supporting information) demonstrated that the CCD and Zn,N-CCDs inhibition efficiencies at the previously indicated concentrations were 87.8 % and 92.8 %, respectively, at 300 and 150 ppm. A further investigation into raising the temperature from 298 K to 303 K results in a 95.83 % increase in efficiency (Fig. 4c and d) and Table S3 (supporting information). As the temperature rises from 313 to 323 K, %\u03B7p falls to 90.69 % and 93.03 %. The %\u03B7P values, however, are nearly identical for all temperatures examined, indicating the applicability of doped CCDs throughout a wide temperature range.The work described in this paper was fully supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52178458); National Science Foundation of Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (Grant No. CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0363 and CSTB2023NSCQ-LZX0051).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis route of CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs.
HRTEM images (a,b) and particle size distribution graphs (c,d) XRD (e) Zeta potential (f) and FT-IR spectra (g) of CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs.

(a,b) E OCP vs. time curves (c,d) Nyquist plots (e,f) Bode plots of Q235B steel in without and with CCDs (b) Zn,N-CCDs at 293 K; the open and solid symbols correspond to the fit of the electrical equivalent circuits and the experimental data respectively, (g,h) Equivalent circuit diagrams used to fit the EIS data (g) blank (h) inhibited solution.
Polarization curves for of Q235B steel at different concentrations of (a) CCDs, (b) Zn,N-CCDs at 293 K, Zn,N-CCDs at (c) 303 K (d) 313 K and 323 K.
(a) XPS-full spectrum, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) S2p (f) Fe2p and (g) Zn2p of CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs.
Weight loss measurement data for Q235B steel in 5 % HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of CDs at 298 K.
Electrochemical impedance parameters for Q235B steel in 5 % HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of undoped and doped CDs.
PDP parameters for Q235B steel in 5 % HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of undoped and doped CCDs.

head.ce:title: Effect of temperature, and immersion time on the inhibition performance of Q235 steel using spent coffee grounds derived carbon quantum dots: Electrochemical, spectroscopic and surface studies

Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the anticorrosive properties of undoped, and zinc and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots derived from spent coffee grounds referred to as CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs respectively, for the protection of Q235B steel corrosion in a 5 % HCl solution. The synthesized carbon quantum dots (CDs) were characterized using Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), zeta potential, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Their anticorrosive performance was assessed through weight loss measurements, Open circuit potential (OCP), Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), Scanning Electron Microscopy/Energy dispersive Spectroscopy (SEM/EDS), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The results demonstrated that Zn,N-CCDs exhibit better inhibition efficiency i.e., 94.65 % at 150 ppm compared to CCDs which show 90.90 % at 300 ppm and 298 K. Moreover, 150 ppm of Zn,N-CCDs at a longer immersion time of up to 72 h at 313 K exhibited an inhibition performance of 98.33 % proving it as an efficient corrosion inhibitor to be used in acid pickling conditions. PDP analysis suggested that the Zn,N-CCDs reduce both anodic and cathodic dissolution processes. XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed the adsorption of CDs through heteroatoms present in the coffee grounds. Furthermore, surface morphological studies indicated significant adsorption of the Zn,N-CCDs on the metal surface, enhancing surface smoothness as compared to CCDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In metallic materials, corrosion is a spontaneous and natural process that causes significant economic losses, estimated to be between 3 and 5 % of GDP [1]. Through the years, several approaches have been used to mitigate the costs associated with corrosion, such as cathodic protection, coatings, sacrificial anodes, corrosion inhibitors, and corrosion-resistant alloys [2,3]. Corrosion inhibitors are unique in their ability to significantly reduce the rate of corrosion [4]. Even when employed in small quantities, these compounds efficiently slow down or prevent corrosion in industries such as oil and gas, aerospace, and construction etc. Organic corrosion inhibitors have attracted interest because of their excellent corrosion inhibition efficacy, affordability, and ease of application [5,6]. To function as adsorption centres on metal or alloy surfaces, heteroatoms like phosphorus, sulfur, or nitrogen are commonly incorporated into the design of organic inhibitors [7]. The protective effect is mainly achieved through chemical or physical adsorption, often facilitated by charge differences between the adsorbed molecules and the metal surface or through charge transfer between the two phases [8,9]. However, the hazardous and relatively high cost of many efficient organic inhibitors has prompted the search for more economical, environmentally safe, and sustainable alternatives. The development of green organic corrosion inhibitors has drawn more attention in recent years [10–12]. The use of carbon quantum dots is also becoming a significant research area in the field of corrosion inhibition [13–15].

Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm are called carbon quantum dots (CDs). Their water solubility, low toxicity, chemical inertness, environmental friendliness, and tunable fluorescence have all drawn a lot of attention since they were discovered by Xu et al., in 2004 [16]. Because of these characteristics, CDs are perfect for use in biosensors, chemical sensing, cell imaging, and medical diagnostics. Generally, techniques like hydrothermal and microwave-assisted approaches are used for producing CDs. The affordability of the hydrothermal method and ease of use, make it an appealing option. However, there are environmental risks associated with other methods like chemical oxidation, pyrolysis or microwave-assisted synthesis because they frequently use powerful acids, substantial quantities of organic solvents, and precursors derived from petroleum. These procedures also use a lot of energy, complicated, and expensive machinery [17].

Using bioresources as precursors for CD synthesis, researchers recently started investigating environmentally friendly alternatives [18]. Although some progress has been made, it is still highly desirable to construct an environmentally benign and highly efficient method for CD synthesis. Spent coffee grounds (SCGs), a leftover byproduct of the coffee brewing process, are a good alternative for this purpose. Since the 2010s, about 6 million tons of SCGs have been produced annually worldwide, primarily due to an increase in coffee consumption [19]. However, an immense quantity of SCGs is thrown away as garbage, which contaminates the ecosystem [20]. Although SCGs are abundant in organic compounds, they can be used as a useful bioresource to produce a variety of products with added value. These products include fatty acids, lignin, cellulose, hemicellulose, polysaccharides, and amino acids [21]. Instead of adding to trash streams, this abundance of organic material presents opportunities for SCGs to be used in a variety of sustainable applications. The process of turning SCGs into useful chemicals including biofuels, and nanomaterials has gained popularity in recent years [22,23]. Their application in the environmentally friendly synthesis of CDs is a viable strategy that supports the objectives of the circular economy and sustainability. Utilizing SCGs, researchers want to minimize environmental impact, save production costs, and lessen dependency on non-renewable resources. Furthermore, adding heteroatoms such as phosphorus, sulfur, or nitrogen to the CDs, introduces active sites that strengthen adsorption onto metal surfaces and provide a barrier that prevents corrosion [24,25]. Doping modifies the electronic structure of CDs, enhancing their capacity for electron transfer which is a critical function in averting the electrochemical processes that result in corrosion. Additionally, doped CDs frequently show enhanced solubility and stability, which increases their effectiveness in a variety of harsh conditions [26]. Identifying environmentally friendly precursors and sustainable synthesis techniques for CDs has become crucial due to the rising need for sustainable materials. As a byproduct of the coffee brewing process, spent coffee grounds (SCGs) have a lot of potential as a renewable precursor.

In contrast to the previous studies focused on using coffee extracts directly [27,28], our work utilizes waste coffee grounds as the source of carbon material to improve its corrosion protection performance. This green and low-cost approach is not only helpful for disposing of waste but also manifests superior inhibition performance, providing a new avenue for exploring green and highly efficient corrosion inhibitors from biowaste resources.

This study reports the synthesis of undoped (CCDs), and Zn and N-doped CDs (Zn,N-CCDs) from waste coffee grounds and assessment of their ability to suppress corrosion of Q235 B steel in a 5 % HCl solution using various experimental techniques, including weight loss measurement, EIS, PDP, UV–vis, FTIR SEM/EDS, and XPS analysis. The goal of the zinc and nitrogen doping was to increase the adsorption capabilities of CDs, which increased their corrosion resistance. The findings support the development of environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors and growing interest in green chemistry.

Roasted coffee bags were purchased from the Nantong Yinhe Trade Co., Ltd, China and brewed to prepare coffee. The resulting spent coffee grounds were collected to use further in the study. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was procured from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. Zinc sulfate heptahydrate and urea were obtained from Xilong Scientific and Tianjin ZhiYuan Reagent Co., Ltd., respectively. Without additional purification, each chemical was used just as it was received. Throughout the experiment, deionized (DI) water was utilized.

The F-7100 FL Hitachi, Japan device was used to measure fluorescence. Quartz cuvettes (2 mL) with a 10 mm optical path and a scanning rate of 1.0 nm encompassing the range from 200 to 800 nm were used to acquire UV–vis absorption spectra using a Thermo Scientific Evolution 300 spectrophotometer. Using a Bruker D8Advance diffractometer, which has a 2θ range of 10°–90°, the XRD data for both samples were obtained. The concentrated solutions of all samples were drop-cast onto cleaned glass slides and dried overnight under vacuum for XRD analysis. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out using a Nicolet IS10 instrument from the USA. For FTIR, lyophilized carbon dots were mixed with dry KBr, pressed into a pellet under 40 MPa pressure for 2 min using a hydraulic press, and analyzed. The hydrothermal reactions were performed in a DGL-125B vacuum drying oven (Tianjin Test Instrument Co., Ltd., China). Utilizing a JEM–2100F TEM from JEOL, Ltd., Tokyo, Japan, operated at 200 keV, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed. Using ImageJ software (Version 1.53, National Institutes of Health, USA), the average particle size and dispersion of carbon dots were determined. To prepare the samples for TEM analysis, the CDs were dispersed in ethanol, ultrasonicated the mixture, and then placed the suspension on a copper TEM grid with a lacey carbon sheet (200 mesh). Using a ThermoFisher K-Alpha device, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted. A Zetasizer Nano ZS90 was used to measure the particle size distribution and zeta potential. Every experiment was run in triplicate, and the mean values were reported.

37 % HCl was diluted with deionized water to yield the 5 % HCl acid solution, which was used in the experiments. The tests were conducted in a thermostatic water bath with a 250 ml corrosive solution at different temperatures. Q235B steel was purchased from the Chaoyang Anling Iron and Steel Co. of the Anshan Iron and Steel Group, China. The composition of the steel wass as follows: C = 0.140 %, Si = 0.20 %, Mn = 0.39 %, P = 0.023 %, S = 0.013 %, and Fe = remaining. The steel sheets were cut into 20 mm × 25 mm × 1 mm coupons, and the samples were abraded using several grades of SiC abrasive sandpaper (ranging from 100 to 7000 grit). Following this, samples were cleaned with acetone and ethanol, rinsed with deionized water, and then dried in the air for weight loss, electrochemical, XPS and surface morphological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of undoped and Zn,N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Zn,N-CCDs and CCDs were produced using a previously reported hydrothermal process [29]. The following steps were included in the synthesis procedure: Initially, moisture was evaporated from spent coffee grounds by drying it at 453 K for 48 h. To prepare Zn,N-CCD, 1 g of dried SCGs served as the carbon source, 0.05 g of urea was employed as the nitrogen source, and 0.05 g of zinc sulfate heptahydrate was used as the zinc source in the Zn,N-CCD production (Fig. 1 ). These precursors were dissolved in 50 ml of deionized water and then placed inside a Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave for 7 h in a drying oven set at 463 K. Following the reaction, the autoclave was let cool to ambient temperature. To eliminate the bigger particles, the resultant mixture was centrifuged for 15 mins at 8000 rpm. The CDs were subsequently purified by filtering the supernatant via a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da) for 24 h, with 4 h intervals of ultrapure water replacement. Ultimately, the Zn,N-CCD were obtained in solid form by vacuum freeze-drying the purified Zn,N-CCD. In contrast, undoped CCDs were produced using the same procedures but without the urea and zinc sulfate additions to compare the effect of Zn and N doping on the carbon dots' ability to suppress corrosion of steel.

To carry out the weight loss measurement, the metal samples were first abraded with SiC emery papers as mentioned above, cleaned with acetone and deionized water, and then allowed to air dry. The samples were weighed with an analytical balance before being immersed in an HCl solution for 6h at different temperatures. After 6h immersion all the samples were carefully cleaned with a soft bristle brush under the tap water to get rid of any corrosion products and then cleaned with acetone and deionized water. The samples were reweighed. In compliance with ASTM G1 and ASTM G31 guidelines, weight loss measurements and corrosion rate calculations were carried out [30,31]. Three runs of the tests were conducted to guarantee data accuracy. The corrosion rates (v) and inhibition efficiency (%η w) were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2).
(1)
ν =
K Δ W
A × t × ρ
(2)
η w
( % )
=
ν 0
−
ν i
ν 0
× 100
where K is a constant with a value of 3.46 × 106,
Δ W
is the weight loss (g), A is the surface area of the steel specimen, t is the immersion time in hour, ρ is the density of steel specimen, ν 0 and ν i denote corrosion rates in the absence and presence of CDs, respectively.

For all electrochemical tests, steel coupons with a 1.0 cm2 exposed area were immersed in the acidic electrolyte. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and polarization tests were also performed using the three-electrode configuration, with a platinum counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and steel as a working electrode. EIS measurements were done over a frequency range of 10−2 Hz–105 Hz with an amplitude of 10 mV versus open-circuit potential (OCP). Polarization measurements were conducted at a scan rate of 1 mV/s, covering a potential range from +250 mV to −250 mV versus OCP. All tests were performed using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd.). The EIS data were analyzed using ZSimDemo software, version 3.3d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic analysis: UV–vis, FTIR and XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: UV–visible absorption spectroscopy was utilized to investigate the potential formation of the inhibitor-metal complex. A 5 % HCl solution containing an optimal inhibitor concentration was tested both before and after immersing Q235B steel samples for 6 h. The analysis was conducted across the wavelength range of 190–1100 nm. Furthermore, the functional groups of both undoped and doped CCDs, as well as the film adhered to the metal surface post-immersion, were examined using the same FT-IR spectrophotometer mentioned previously. Samples were prepared as mentioned in the weight loss measurement section, and the film formed on the working electrode was scraped off, mixed with KBr, converted to pellets, and subsequently subjected to FT-IR analysis.

Utilizing a Thermo Scientific Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi from the United States, an X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) examination was performed to ascertain the corrosion inhibitors' surface chemical composition. The Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) was utilized, and the binding energies were calibrated using C1s (284.8 eV). Following a 15-h pre-chambering period, the cleaned specimens were moved to the analytical chamber, which was kept at a base pressure of 1 × 10−9 Torr. Following sputtering, the samples were exposed to Ar ion etching for 120 s before XPS measurements were performed.

For FESEM/EDS analysis, the Q235B steel specimens were studied after 6 h immersion in 5 % HCl at 293 K in the presence and absence of the inhibitors. After cleaning the samples as described above, scanning electron microscope (SEM) analysis was conducted using an SU801 instrument from Hitachi Ltd., Japan, equipped with an inbuilt energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analyzer to perform EDS studies.
After the synthesis of the undoped and doped CDs, the systemic characterization was done using the following methods:

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was carried out to determine the morphology of the CCDs. Fig. 2 shows the HRTEM images for CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs. The synthesized undoped and Zn,N-CCDs were confirmed to have a spherical shape (Fig. 2a and b) and an average size of 9.8 nm, as shown in the TEM image. The size of CCDs (Fig. 2c) was found to be slightly bigger than that of Zn,N-CCDs (12.7 nm) (Fig. 2d). The smaller particle size contributes to the increased denseness of the particles in doped CCDs [32,33].

The XRD pattern (Fig. 2e) of the CCDs (black curve) exhibits a broad diffraction peak located at approximately 27.91°, which is well consistent with the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (JCPDS No. 41–1487) [34], suggesting an amorphous carbon structure. In contrast, Zn,N-CCDs (red curve) show several additional sharp diffraction peaks at 9.4°, 19,36°, 28.14° and 30.22°, which can be indexed to the (001), (002), (100) planes of Zn-O, respectively [35]. These results suggest successful doping and possible crystallization of Zn-related phases within the carbon matrix.

Zeta potential measures the degree of electrostatic repulsion between similarly charged particles in a dispersed system. The zeta potential values for CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs are shown in Fig. 2f. A negative zeta potential indicates repulsive electrostatic forces between the molecules. The greater the negative charge, the stronger the repulsive forces, resulting in a more stable dispersed system compared to the pure system [36]. The zeta potential value of CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs was determined to be −5.83 mV and −10.2 mV, respectively, indicating the presence of negatively charged surface groups such as carboxylates or hydroxyl groups. From the zeta potential diagram (Fig. 2f), it is observed that the charge strength of CCDs (−5.83 mV) was lower compared to Zn,N-CCDs, indicating its relatively good stability and dispersity in aqueous solution. A more negative zeta potential typically indicates greater repulsion between particles, leading to increased stability and reduced aggregation [37,38].

The functional groups of CDs were successfully identified by FT-IR spectrum analysis. According to Fig. 2g, a large peak at 3428 cm−3 represents hydroxyl groups due to -OH stretching. A possible indicator of C-H bonding is the absorption band at about 2931 and 2870 cm−1 [39]. Sharp peaks at 1779 cm−1 are linked to carboxyl groups (C=O) [40], whereas -CONH groups are associated with the peak at 1663 cm−1. Bending vibrations of -N-H are indicated as peaks at approximately 1417 cm−1. An extra peak indicates the Zn-O bonds in Zn,N-CCDs at 615 cm−1 [41].

When placed on a UV lamp (λ max:365 nm, model ZF-1, Shanghai Lichenbangxi Instrument Technology Co., Ltd., China), both the Zn,N-CCDs and CCDs samples displayed a cyan colour (Fig. S1a–b, supporting information). Afterwards, the optical analysis of the synthesized CCDs was also analyzed using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopic methods. The UV–vis spectroscopy shows a characteristic absorption peak at 294 nm and 299 nm for CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs, respectively due to the electronic transition from π to π∗ of the sp2 hybridized carbon−heteroatom bond (C=O and C=N) [42] (Fig. S1c–d, supporting information). Fig. S1e–f (supporting information) presents the changes in fluorescence emission intensities of CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs at various excitations ranging from 280 nm to 500 nm in 10 nm increments. The fluorescence spectra exhibited the highest emission peaks at 443 nm for CCDs and 415 nm for Zn-N CCDs, with excitations at 360 nm and 340 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight loss measurement: effect of concentration, temperature and immersion time
ce:section.ce:para: The weight loss data for the inhibition performance was collected in the presence and absence of CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs at a constant temperature of 298 K (Table 1 ). The study aimed to analyze how the inhibition performance of both types of CCDs varied with increasing doses. Notably, the effect was more prominent in the doped CCDs, which exhibited enhanced performance due to metal and non-metal doping. This enhancement is attributed to the synergistic effect of the dopants, which are likely to provide additional active sites and improve the adsorption characteristics of the CDs on the metal surface.

Given the superior inhibition performance of the doped CDs, further investigation was conducted to examine the effect of temperature variations from 303 K to 323 K on the corrosion rate of Q235B steel in HCl solution, at different concentrations of the doped CDs. As illustrated in Table S1 (supporting information), the corrosion rate of Q235B steel increased with rising temperature, which is expected due to the higher kinetic energy of molecules at elevated temperatures. At 303 K, the corrosion rate was 202 mpy, which increased to 1283 mpy at 333 K. Introducing doped CDs into the HCl solution reduced the corrosion rate compared to the uninhibited solution. This reduction was more significant as the concentration of doped CDs increased from 10 ppm to 150 ppm at 303 K. Higher concentrations yielded only slight improvements in inhibition efficiency due to the saturation of active sites on the metal surface. Inhibition efficiency decreased as the temperature rose from 303 K to 323 K, likely due to the desorption of CD molecules from the metal surface.

To assess the effect of extended immersion on inhibition performance, Zn,N-CCDs were tested at the optimal concentration of 150 ppm at 313 K. Results showed that the inhibition performance improved up to 72 h of immersion at the studied temperature, with only a slight decrease from 72 to 96 h (Fig. S2, supporting information). This indicates the continued effectiveness of the CDs prepared over longer periods and their suitability for acid-cleaning operations.

OCP, potentiodynamic polarization, and EIS measurements were utilized to assess the corrosion inhibition efficacy of undoped and doped CDs for Q235B steel in 5 % HCl. As the CCDs concentration increases, the OCP graphs in Fig. 3 a–b demonstrates that OCP values shift to less negative potentials, indicating that anodic reactions are more affected by CCDs than cathodic ones. After approximately 600 s, each sample attained steady-state conditions, at which point additional electrochemical studies were carried out. The adsorption of CCDs and the capacitive behavior of the steel electrode at different CCD concentrations were investigated using EIS. Fig. 3c-f shows the Nyquist and Bode plots for steel specimens in 5 % HCl with and without CCDs. A single depressed capacitive semicircle is shown in the Nyquist plots, which suggests that charge transfer oversees the corrosion process [42]. The radii of the Nyquist plots increased with increasing concentrations of CDs, as seen in Fig. 3c-d.

This is probably because CCDs adsorb the metal/electrolyte interfaces. Fig. 3(g and h) show the equivalent circuits utilized to fit the experimental impedance data which includes the solution resistance (R s), charge transfer resistance (R ct), dispersion coefficient (n), and constant phase elements (CPEf and CPEdl). Table 2 summarizes the EIS parameters. The findings show that the Q235B steel immersed in doped CDs shows the highest R ct (428 Ω cm2) compared to the blank (27.03 Ω cm2) and Q235B steel with undoped CDs (238.4 Ω cm2). Y 0 and n define the frequency-dependent CPE, which was introduced to account for surface roughness [43]. The impedance of the constant phase element (CPE) can be calculated using Eq. (3) [44].
(3)
Z
C P E
=
(
Y 0
)
− 1
(
j ω
)
− n

Z CPE stands for the CPE impedance; Y 0 is the CPE constant; j is the imaginary unit; n is the dispersion coefficient; and ω is the angular frequency. The frequency dispersion effect is reflected in the value of n, which also quantifies surface roughness, inhibitor adsorption, and surface inhomogeneity brought on by the creation of porous layers.

Given that all n values are less than 1, the system's dispersion effects may result from uneven electrode surfaces or irregular current distribution at the electrode/solution interface [45,46]. Eqs. (4) and (5) were used to determine the double-layer capacitance (C dl) and inhibitory efficiency values [47].
(4)
C
d l
=
Y 0
(
ω max
)
n − 1
(5)
η E
( % )
=
(
1 −
R t 0
R t
)
× 100
where
ω max
= 2πf max, and f max is the frequency at which the imaginary component of the impedance is maximal,
R
t o
,
R t
, are the resistances of steel in the absence and presence of a varying concentration of undoped and doped CCDs, respectively.

The consistent decline in C dl, Y dl, and Y f values with rising CDs concentration suggests the adsorbed layer becomes thicker and more effective, covering more of the MS surface, deactivating active sites, and significantly slowing down corrosion [48]. Significantly, R ct and R t values are higher while C dl, Y dl, and Y f values are lower in the presence of doped CDs than in undoped CDs, indicating that the former adsorbs on metal surfaces more efficiently. Table 2 and S2 (supporting information) demonstrate that the R ct is substantially higher than the blank at all CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs concentrations. As the inhibitor concentration rises, there is a discernible increase in both R ct and %η E. The highest corrosion efficiencies obtained with CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs were 88.71 % and 93.70 %, respectively. Because Zn and N elements are doped, Zn,N-CCDs show better corrosion resistance than undoped CDs. Bode plots (Fig. 3e-f) demonstrate that CCDs effectively limit corrosion at all frequencies, with the |Z| at 0.01 Hz increasing significantly relative to the blank and phase angle values becoming more negative [49].

The effect of temperature on the steel corrosion without and 150 ppm of Zn,N-CCDs was further examined, and the corresponding Nyquist and Bode plots are shown in Fig. S3(a–d) (supporting information). At 303 K, both R ct increased, and C dl decreased. However, at higher temperatures, the opposite trend was observed, indicating potential dissolution of the steel surface due to the desorption of CDs from the metal surface.

The inhibition efficiency of undoped and doped CDs for Q235B steel in 5 % HCl at 298 K was measured using potentiodynamic polarization experiments (Fig. 4 a and b) and then inhibition performances of 150 ppm of Zn,N-CCDs were evaluated at 303, 313 and 333 K (Fig. 4c–e). Table 3 summarizes the findings for the steel specimens without and with undoped and doped CDs including inhibition efficiency (%η p), corrosion potential (E corr), cathodic slope (β c), anodic slope (β a), and corrosion current density (I corr). In the presence of CDs, a positive shift in E corr was observed as compared to the blank, showing that the CDs are effectively inhibiting the process. When samples were immersed in CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs, the E corr values were −0.445 V and −0.461 V versus Ag/AgCl, respectively. For MS, the E corr was −0.455 V vs Ag/AgCl. For CCDs and Zn,N-CCDs, the I corr values considerably decreases from 8.95 × 10−4 A cm−2 (blank) to 0.65 × 10−4 and 0.46 × 10−4 A cm−2, respectively. At 200 ppm, the Zn,N-CCDs sample showed the maximum corrosion inhibition efficacies i.e., 91.9 %. Furthermore, in the presence of inhibitors, the anodic (β a) and cathodic (β c) slopes were reduced. A reduced surface area for hydrogen evolution is shown by the decrease in β c, whilst diminished anodic dissolution is indicated by the reduction in β a. Eq. (6) was utilized to compute the efficiency of corrosion inhibition [49].
(6)
η p
( % )
=
(
1 −
I
c o r r
I
c o r r
o
)
× 100
Where
I
c o r r
o
, and
I
c o r r
are the corrosion current densities of steel in the absence and presence of a varying concentration of CDs, respectively.

The information provided in Table 3 and S3 (supporting information) demonstrated that the CCD and Zn,N-CCDs inhibition efficiencies at the previously indicated concentrations were 87.8 % and 92.8 %, respectively, at 300 and 150 ppm. A further investigation into raising the temperature from 298 K to 303 K results in a 95.83 % increase in efficiency (Fig. 4c and d) and Table S3 (supporting information). As the temperature rises from 313 to 323 K, %η p falls to 90.69 % and 93.03 %. The %η P values, however, are nearly identical for all temperatures examined, indicating the applicability of doped CCDs throughout a wide temperature range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic analysis: UV–vis, FTIR and XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The UV spectra of the solution in which the steel specimen was immersed in the presence of doped carbon dots exhibited two peaks, at 202 and 280 nm, compared to the single peal at 299 nm in the pure doped CDs which suggests significant interaction between carbon dots and the Q235B steel surface. The peak at 299 nm in the pure CDs is characteristic of π →π∗ transitions in the conjugated structure of CDs (Fig. S4a, supporting information). From the after-immersion result, two absorption peaks were observed π→π∗ (202.62 nm) and n→π∗ (280.17 nm), indicating the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface [33]. The presence of an additional peak in the spectra of adsorbed CDs on the Q235B steel surface suggests the formation of new complexes or coordination entities. This interaction leads to enhanced corrosion resistance of Q235B steel.

The pure CCDs exhibit distinct peaks representing various functional groups, as detailed in the section ‘characterization methods (Fig. 2g) exhibiting the peaks around 3428, 2080, 1779, 1663, 1417, and 615 cm−1, which signifies OH, C-H, C=O, CONH, N–H, and ZnO functional groups respectively [37], indicating the presence of N, O, and Zn elements on the surface of CDs. Upon immersion of steel in a 5 % HCl solution containing CDs, noticeable alterations are observed in the FT-IR spectrum, as depicted in Fig. S4b, (supporting information). For instance, the broadband peak at 3428 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of CCDs, associated with OH stretching, shifts to 3423 cm−1 post-immersion. The intensity of bands between 1779 and 1663 cm−1 in the initial IR spectrum, correlated with C=O and CONH stretching modes, notably decreases to 1640 and 1512 cm−1 respectively, as shown in Fig. S4b (supporting information). This suggests that oxygen atoms in Coffee-derived CDs may coordinate with Fe2+ ions, forming complexes. Moreover, the bands initially observed in the CCDs spectra at 1417 cm−1 for N–H experience a shift to higher wavenumbers, registering at 1452 cm−1 (post-immersion). These changes in the FTIR spectrum indicate potential interactions between CCDs and the steel surface, hinting at the formation of coordination complexes that may aid in the corrosion inhibition process.

XPS analysis assesses the elemental composition and chemical bonds in CDs. The adsorption of CD molecules on the Q235B steel/electrolyte interface was evaluated using XPS analysis. The full survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS graphs are shown in Fig. 5 a-g. Fig. 5a revealed the presence of carbon (47.85 %At.), nitrogen (5.35 %At.), iodine (5.49 %At.), and chlorine (5.60 %At.), as well as iron (14.09 %At.) and oxygen (21.71 %At.), indicating successful adsorption of the CDs on the immersed Q235B steel surface.

C1s spectra exhibit multiple peaks due to various chemical environments of carbon dots. Around 284 eV show graphitic carbon Fig. 5b. The peak at 286 eV corresponds to carbon in hydroxyl or ether groups (C-O). The peak at 287–288 eV is associated with carbonyl groups (C=O) [50–52]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the XPS spectra of N1s showed the peak around 398.8–398.7 eV corresponds to nitrogen atoms bonded to two carbon atoms as in pyridinic nitrogen, while the peak around 400 eV is responsible for three carbon atoms typical of pyrrolic nitrogen. The peak around 401 eV shows the formation of the N-Fe bond. The peak of O2s shows (Fig. 5d), three peaks: 530 eV (for carbonyl oxygen, C=O), 531 eV (for hydroxyl and ether groups, C-OH/C-O-C) and 532 eV (carboxyl groups, O-C=O) [53]. Fig. 5e shows the four deconvoluted S2p peaks at 162.1 eV, 163.5 eV, 168.02 eV and 169.54 eV that correspond to S-Fe, C = S/C-S, and –C-S(O) x -C, respectively [54]. Fig. 5f shows the XPS spectra of Fe2p which was deconvoluted in four peaks at 710.5 eV, 713.8 eV, 723.5 eV, and 725.7 eV. The first two peaks are associated with Fe 2p3/2 core level and second two peaks are associated with Fe 2p1/2. The peak around 710.5 eV is attributed to Fe2+ or Fe3+ due to the FeO or Fe2O3 or Fe- N [55]. The peak at 713 eV suggests the presence of Fe3+ in Fe2O3 indicating some degree of oxidation. The presence of these peaks suggest that the Q235B steel surface immersed in Zn, N-CCDS contains iron in both Fe2+ and Fe3+ oxidation states. This indicates that there is a mixture of iron oxides on the surface. An additional peak of Zn was observed in the XPS spectra of doped CCDs. The Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks were observed at 1020 and 1039 eV, as given in Fig. 5g. This could be ascribed to the interaction between the Zn atoms and the underlying MS substrate [54,56].

SEM was used to analyze the microstructure of Q235B steel surfaces both with and without inhibitors. When Q235B steel was subjected to 5 % HCl without an inhibitor, it showed pitting because of a chloride ion attack (Fig. S5a, supporting information). As a visual indicator of the inhibitors' efficacy, Q235B steel samples in undoped (Fig. S5b, supporting information) and doped CDs (Fig. S5c, supporting information) showed a consistently smooth surface.

The iron percentage on Q235B steel surfaces in 5 % HCl, both with and without inhibitors, was measured by EDX analysis (Fig. S6a–d, supporting information). 84.14 % iron was present in the Q235B steel specimen without an inhibitor (Fig. S6a, supporting information). Iron content improved to 91.34 % in samples immersed in CCDs (Fig. S6b, supporting information) and to 94.94 % in samples immersed in Zn,N-CCDs (Fig. S6c, supporting information). Compared to undoped CCDs, Zn,N-CCDs retained the highest amount of iron on the metal surface, indicating that it is the most effective inhibitor.

The study demonstrates that undoped and Zn,N-doped CDs derived from spent coffee grounds significantly enhance the corrosion resistance of Q235B steel in a 5 % HCl solution. Characterization techniques, including TEM, XRD, zeta potential, FT-IR, and fluorescence spectroscopy, confirmed the successful synthesis and functional properties of the doped CDs. The anti-corrosive performance, evaluated through weight loss, OCP, EIS, PDP, SEM, and XPS techniques, revealed that Zn,N-CCDs provide better inhibition efficiency than undoped CDs. Surface morphological studies supported the effective adsorption of doped CDs on the metal surface, improving surface smoothness and integrity. XPS and FTIR spectra confirmed the adsorption mechanism through the presence of heteroatoms. The PDP results identified the mixed-type behaviour of the doped CDs, inhibiting both anodic and cathodic reactions. Thus, Zn,N-CCDs from spent coffee grounds emerge as promising, eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for Q235B steel in acidic environments.

Ruby Aslam: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qihui Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Jeenat Aslam: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Mohammad Mobin: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Chaudhery Mustansar Hussain: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Zhitao Yan: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this paper was fully supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52178458); National Science Foundation of Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (Grant No. CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0363 and CSTB2023NSCQ-LZX0051).

ce:section.ce:para:
